content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Adversarial Prompting to Mitigate Stereotypes in LLM Outputs",
  "problem": "Large language models frequently replicate social biases and stereotypes in their outputs, even when prompted for neutrality. Existing prompting methods often rely on explicit instructions or debiasing templates, which can be circumvented or ignored.",
  "motivation": "Mitigating bias at inference time is crucial as LLMs are commonly deployed in sensitive, high-impact domains. Prior work assumes that instructing models to avoid bias suffices, but recent evidence shows that models still leak stereotypes, especially in subtle or ambiguous contexts.",
  "method": "We propose 'counterfactual adversarial prompting,' in which each user query is automatically paired with a minimally perturbed counterfactual version (e.g., swapping demographic attributes or occupations). Both prompts are fed to the LLM, and the outputs are compared in real time. If the outputs exhibit asymmetric stereotypical content, the system regenerates or edits responses until parity is achieved, guided by a learned stereotype detector. This prompting framework is model-agnostic and operates without access to LLM internals.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on bias-detection benchmarks (e.g., StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs) and open-domain question-answering datasets. Baselines include standard instruction-based debiasing and prompt engineering techniques. Metrics will include stereotype leakage rate, answer consistency across counterfactuals, and response utility (measured by human raters)."
}
